page: Amazon Web Services x2iedn.2xlarge is a memory-optimized instance built on the Nitro hypervisor. It features 8 vCPUs and 4 physical cores powered by an Intel Xeon 8375C processor running at 3.5 GHz. The system architecture is x86_64, utilizing hyper-threading with 2 threads per core. Memory capacity is 256.0 GB of DDR4 RAM, translating to 32.0 GB per physical core. Local storage is provided via a bundled 237 GB NVMe SSD instance store volume, and the baseline network bandwidth is 5 Gbps. No GPUs are included in this configuration.

Benchmark data indicates average performance across most compute and memory operations. The instance achieved a Geekbench single-core score of 1647.0 and a multi-core score of 6078.0, alongside a Passmark CPU score of 11305.6. Memory read-only bandwidth reached 414.09 GB/sec for cached small blocks and 58.31 GB/sec for uncached large blocks. Database operations on Passmark scored 3929.1, while Redis SET operations reached 7.93 million operations per second.

While the instance maintains average performance for standard tasks, certain multi-threaded and large-scale workloads show performance limitations. Specifically, bzip3 multi-threaded decompression is weak, falling into the bottom 25% of benchmarks with a score of 32.18 GB/sec. Additionally, LLM inference prompt processing for medium 7B models is poor, registering at 16.29 tokens/sec. Given the high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 32 GB per core and the bundled NVMe storage, this instance is qualitatively cost-effective for memory-intensive workloads such as in-memory databases, caching layers, and enterprise applications rather than heavy parallel compute or large language model processing.
description: The Amazon Web Services x2iedn.2xlarge is an x86_64 memory-optimized instance running on the Nitro hypervisor. It is equipped with an Intel Xeon 8375C processor providing 8 vCPUs and 4 physical cores at 3.5 GHz, 256.0 GB of DDR4 RAM, and a 237 GB local NVMe SSD. Designed for memory-intensive applications, it delivers 32.0 GB of RAM per core and a baseline network bandwidth of 5 Gbps. Benchmarks show average performance across standard compute, memory bandwidth, and database operations, including 7.93 million Redis SET operations per second. However, tradeoffs exist in multi-threaded decompression and medium-sized LLM inference prompt processing, which perform below average. This configuration is best suited for in-memory databases, caching layers, and memory-heavy enterprise workloads where high memory density is required relative to compute resources.
